heroOverview: Hypovolemia is a deficit of circulating blood volume caused by the loss of blood, plasma, or body fluids exceeding the body's ability to compensate. Even a 10% reduction in effective circulating volume triggers measurable compensatory responses — tachycardia, peripheral vasoconstriction, and reduced urine output — as the cardiovascular system strains to maintain perfusion to vital organs. Severe cases, defined by a loss of more than 30% of circulating volume, can produce haemorrhagic or distributive shock within minutes. The condition spans a wide spectrum: mild hypovolemia from vomiting or poor oral intake is remedied with oral fluids, while severe trauma-, burn-, or sepsis-related losses require emergency resuscitation with intravenous crystalloids or blood products. Early recognition is critical because the clinical deterioration from compensation to shock can be abrupt.

keyStats.prevalence: Hypovolemic shock affects approximately 1-2% of all hospital admissions in high-income countries; dehydration contributing to clinically significant hypovolemia is among the top causes of emergency department visits globally
keyStats.demographics: All age groups are affected; extremes of age (infants and elderly) are at highest risk due to limited physiological reserve
keyStats.avgAge: No peak age; risk is greatest in trauma patients (young adults), elderly with reduced fluid intake, and critically ill patients
keyStats.globalCases: Haemorrhagic shock alone causes an estimated 1.9 million deaths worldwide annually, according to WHO injury burden data

definition: Hypovolemia (ICD-10: E86.1) is an abnormal decrease in the volume of blood plasma or total circulating blood volume, impairing the delivery of oxygen and nutrients to tissues. The term encompasses both haemorrhage (loss of whole blood) and dehydration states in which fluid and electrolyte losses reduce plasma volume. Pathophysiologically, the reduction in preload decreases cardiac output; baroreceptors at the aortic arch and carotid sinus detect falling arterial pressure and trigger compensatory sympathetic outflow, raising heart rate and systemic vascular resistance to maintain mean arterial pressure. When losses are moderate to severe, these mechanisms become insufficient, and end-organ hypoperfusion begins — first the kidneys (oliguria), then the splanchnic circulation, and ultimately, in class III-IV shock, the brain and heart. Classification by the American College of Surgeons Advanced Trauma Life Support system stratifies haemorrhagic hypovolemia into four classes based on estimated blood loss: class I (up to 15% of blood volume) through class IV (above 40%), each with corresponding physiological derangements. Non-haemorrhagic hypovolemia arises from gastrointestinal losses (vomiting, diarrhoea), renal losses (diuretic overuse, uncontrolled diabetes, adrenal insufficiency), cutaneous losses (burns, sweating), and third-space shifts (pancreatitis, sepsis, major surgery). Hypovolemia falls under the management domain of emergency medicine, critical care, and endocrinology when the cause involves hormonal regulation of fluid balance.

typesClassification.description: Caused by loss of whole blood from trauma, surgical bleeding, or gastrointestinal haemorrhage; classified into four ATLS classes by estimated blood loss percentage.
Fluid and electrolyte deficits from vomiting, diarrhoea, inadequate oral intake, or excessive sweating reduce plasma volume without direct blood loss.

Occurs when fluid shifts from the intravascular to the extravascular compartment, as seen in sepsis, severe burns, and anaphylaxis, where capillary leak depletes circulating volume even when total body water is normal or elevated.
Excessive urinary fluid losses from diuretics, osmotic diuresis in uncontrolled diabetes, or adrenal insufficiency (mineralocorticoid deficiency) drain intravascular volume.

Large fluid volumes are sequestered in extravascular compartments — such as the bowel lumen in ileus, the peritoneum in pancreatitis, or oedematous tissues in major burns — making them physiologically unavailable.

primarySymptoms: Tachycardia — a resting heart rate above 100 beats per minute is often the earliest objective sign, as the heart accelerates to maintain cardiac output in the face of falling preload.
Hypotension — a systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a drop of more than 30 mmHg from baseline indicates loss of compensatory capacity and impending shock.
Decreased urine output — the kidneys retain fluid aggressively; output below 0.5 mL/kg/hour in adults is a sensitive early warning of inadequate renal perfusion.
Dizziness and lightheadedness — especially on standing (orthostatic hypotension), reflecting the inability of the cardiovascular system to maintain cerebral perfusion on position change.
Dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor — visible signs of extracellular fluid depletion, most reliable in children; skin tenting over the abdomen or forearm suggests significant loss.
Cool, pale, or mottled extremities — peripheral vasoconstriction shunts blood to vital organs, leaving the limbs cold to touch and poorly perfused.
Altered mental status — confusion, restlessness, and agitation reflect cerebral underperfusion and are ominous signs in the context of suspected volume loss.
Capillary refill time prolonged beyond 2 seconds — a bedside test indicating reduced peripheral perfusion due to vasoconstriction.
Thirst — a conscious patient with hypovolemia will experience intense thirst driven by osmoreceptor activation and angiotensin II signalling.
Rapid shallow breathing — compensatory tachypnoea occurs as the body attempts to increase oxygen delivery in the setting of reduced cardiac output.
Flat neck veins when supine — collapsed jugular veins indicate low central venous pressure and depleted right-heart filling volume.
Syncope or pre-syncope — loss of consciousness or near-fainting on standing reflects the inability to compensate for positional reduction in venous return.
earlyWarningSigns: A slight increase in resting heart rate above the person's baseline, especially after mild exertion or change of position.
Darker urine than usual combined with less frequent urination, suggesting early renal fluid conservation.
Mild lightheadedness when rising quickly from sitting or lying, indicating borderline orthostatic hypotension.
Dry mouth and reduced saliva production even with ambient temperatures and activity levels that would not normally cause dehydration.
emergencySigns: Systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg or a heart rate above 120 beats per minute at rest — indicating class III or IV haemorrhagic shock.
Loss of consciousness or acute confusion accompanying rapid blood loss, such as from trauma or gastrointestinal haemorrhage.
Visible major haemorrhage — wounds with arterial spurting, haematemesis with clots, or melaena suggesting massive gastrointestinal bleed.
Mottled, cold extremities with prolonged capillary refill above 3 seconds and absent peripheral pulses.
Any infant or child with sunken fontanelle, absent tears, markedly reduced skin turgor, and no urine output for over 6 hours.

causes.description: Blunt or penetrating trauma lacerates blood vessels and solid organs, releasing large volumes rapidly; surgical procedures carry blood loss risk proportional to complexity and the vascularity of the operative field. A 70 kg adult has approximately 5 litres of blood, and losing 1.5 litres (class III) begins to overwhelm compensation.

Severe vomiting or diarrhoea from gastroenteritis, cholera, or bowel obstruction can deplete several litres of isotonic or hypotonic fluid within hours, with co-occurring electrolyte losses that worsen cardiovascular stability and the ability of the kidneys to retain volume.

Thermal injury disrupts capillary integrity over wide areas, causing large volumes of plasma protein and fluid to leak from the intravascular space into the interstitium and blister fluid. Burns exceeding 20% of total body surface area require structured intravenous resuscitation using formulae such as the Parkland formula (4 mL/kg per % burn in 24 hours).

The systemic inflammatory response to infection releases cytokines such as TNF-alpha and IL-6 that increase capillary permeability, redistributing litres of plasma-equivalent fluid into the extravascular space within hours; this distributive mechanism underlies the volume depletion of septic shock even before direct fluid losses occur.

Loop diuretics given in excess, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus producing osmotic diuresis, diabetes insipidus, or Addison's disease with aldosterone deficiency all cause abnormal renal fluid wasting that progressively depletes intravascular volume if intake does not compensate.

Acute pancreatitis, bowel obstruction, peritonitis, and major abdominal surgery cause massive fluid translocation into the retroperitoneum, bowel wall, and peritoneal cavity. Several litres can be sequestered within 12-24 hours, creating haemodynamic compromise despite intact skin and no visible external loss.

Elderly patients, infants, or acutely ill individuals unable to drink adequately develop insidious depletion as obligatory daily losses of approximately 1.5-2.5 litres (urine, sweat, respiration) are not replaced, causing a gradual fall in effective circulating volume over days.

riskFactors.description: Older adults have reduced thirst sensation, diminished renal concentrating ability, lower lean body mass, and blunted cardiovascular compensation, making them prone to rapid haemodynamic compromise from volume losses that younger people would tolerate.

Infants have a higher surface-area-to-volume ratio, greater obligatory insensible losses, and limited ability to communicate thirst; gastroenteritis can produce life-threatening hypovolemia within hours in this age group.
Poorly controlled diabetes causes osmotic diuresis that can deplete intravascular volume substantially; the combination with nephropathy reduces renal compensatory capacity further.

Loop and thiazide diuretics prescribed at excessive doses, or laxative misuse, produce ongoing renal or gastrointestinal fluid losses that accumulate into significant hypovolemia, particularly in elderly patients with limited reserve.

Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) causes mineralocorticoid deficiency; without aldosterone, the kidneys cannot retain sodium and water adequately, and volume depletion with hyponatraemia results, often precipitated by illness or stress.

riskFactors.factor: High ambient temperature or strenuous exercise
riskFactors.description: Sweat losses during prolonged exercise in heat can exceed 2 litres per hour; without adequate fluid replacement, athletes and outdoor workers accumulate progressive hypovolemia that can culminate in heat stroke.

Loss of tubular concentrating mechanisms in advanced kidney disease reduces the ability to conserve water and sodium, making patients vulnerable to volume depletion during intercurrent illness or reduced oral intake.

Injury severity, operative complexity, and the inflammatory response to tissue damage all drive fluid losses; patients undergoing major abdominal or cardiothoracic surgery require careful intraoperative and postoperative volume management.

affectedDemographics: Trauma patients, predominantly male young adults aged 15-44 (the leading demographic for haemorrhagic shock)
Elderly adults over 75 with reduced physiological reserve and poor oral intake
Infants under 2 years with gastroenteritis or diarrhoeal illness
Burn patients of any age with burns exceeding 15-20% total body surface area
Patients with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus developing osmotic diuresis
Patients with Addison's disease or other causes of mineralocorticoid deficiency
Athletes, outdoor workers, and military personnel in hot environments
Postoperative patients after major abdominal, cardiac, or orthopaedic surgery
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of hypovolemia is clinical first and laboratory-supported second. History and examination identify likely causes and severity: a patient with trauma, burns, or haematemesis has an obvious source; an elderly patient with confusion, dry mucous membranes, and dark urine presents a subtler picture. Vital signs are central — tachycardia is sensitive but non-specific; hypotension is a late sign that implies compensation has failed. Orthostatic vital signs (a drop in systolic BP of more than 20 mmHg or an increase in heart rate of more than 20 beats per minute on standing) detect moderate hypovolemia in the ambulatory patient. Urine output measured via catheter provides continuous haemodynamic information in critically ill patients. Laboratory assessment confirms and quantifies: a rising serum urea-to-creatinine ratio reflects prerenal azotaemia; urine sodium below 20 mmol/L and urine osmolality above 500 mOsm/kg indicate appropriate renal sodium retention; haematocrit rises in dehydration but falls in haemorrhage. Lactate is a critical marker — a level above 4 mmol/L with haemodynamic instability indicates class IV shock and predicts high mortality without immediate resuscitation. Serial lactate measurements guide resuscitation adequacy. Dynamic tests of fluid responsiveness — passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation on mechanical ventilation, or point-of-care echocardiography assessing inferior vena cava collapsibility — guide fluid therapy in the ICU, distinguishing volume-depleted from distributive shock states.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect tachycardia, hypotension, and the positional drop in BP that signals moderate volume depletion before lying-down BP falls.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nurse takes blood pressure and pulse lying, then standing; a significant drop or heart rate rise indicates volume depletion. Continuous monitoring is set up in severe cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assess renal perfusion (raised urea/creatinine ratio indicates prerenal state), electrolyte derangements, and baseline kidney function guiding fluid choice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results return within 1-2 hours in most hospitals. Repeated every 4-6 hours during active resuscitation to track response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify the degree of tissue hypoperfusion; lactate above 2 mmol/L suggests tissue oxygen debt and rises with severity; clearance of lactate over time confirms resuscitation adequacy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood from the same draw or an arterial line; a bedside point-of-care analyser provides results in minutes, making it standard in emergency and ICU settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Haematocrit rises in dehydration (haemoconcentration) but may be normal or low immediately after acute haemorrhage before haemodilution occurs; haemoglobin guides transfusion decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the standard blood draw; results within 1-2 hours, though acute haemorrhage can be missed by haemoglobin alone in the first hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine sodium, osmolality, and output measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Low urine sodium and high osmolality confirm the kidney is retaining sodium and water appropriately, supporting a prerenal cause rather than intrinsic kidney damage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urinary catheter is placed in critically ill patients for continuous output measurement; a spot urine sample from a catheter or midstream collection is sent to the laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid bedside assessment of cardiac filling, inferior vena cava calibre and collapsibility, and cardiac function to guide volume replacement and distinguish haemorrhagic from distributive or cardiogenic causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe placed on the chest and abdomen takes 5-10 minutes; used at the bedside in emergency and ICU settings without patient preparation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Temporarily mobilises 150-300 mL of venous blood from the legs into the central circulation; an increase in cardiac output or pulse pressure greater than 10-15% predicts that the patient will respond to further intravenous fluid.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient's legs are raised to 45 degrees for 60-90 seconds while heart rate, blood pressure, or cardiac output is measured continuously; no needles or equipment beyond monitoring are required.

treatmentOverview: Treatment targets the simultaneous restoration of effective circulating volume, correction of the underlying cause, and prevention of end-organ damage. In mild to moderate dehydration-associated hypovolemia in a conscious patient, oral rehydration with water and electrolytes is first-line and highly effective, especially in paediatric gastroenteritis where WHO oral rehydration solution (ORS) — 75 mmol/L sodium, 75 mmol/L glucose — reduces mortality more effectively than many intravenous regimens. When the oral route is unsafe or losses are too rapid, intravenous therapy is required. Isotonic crystalloids — 0.9% sodium chloride or balanced solutions such as Hartmann's (lactated Ringer's) or Plasmalyte — are the cornerstone of initial volume replacement; current evidence from multiple trials, including the SMART and SALT-ED studies, supports balanced crystalloids over normal saline because they reduce the risk of hyperchloraemic acidosis and acute kidney injury. For haemorrhagic shock, early haemostasis — surgical, endovascular, or by direct pressure — is the definitive intervention; permissive hypotension (targeting a systolic BP of 80-90 mmHg before haemostasis is achieved) limits coagulopathy from aggressive crystalloid use. Damage-control resuscitation with packed red blood cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio has transformed trauma outcomes by treating traumatic coagulopathy early. Tranexamic acid given within 3 hours of injury reduces haemorrhage-related death, as shown in the landmark CRASH-2 trial. Burns resuscitation uses the Parkland formula but with titration to urine output. Vasopressors such as noradrenaline are added in distributive shock when volume alone fails to maintain an adequate MAP, but they are secondary to volume correction rather than a substitute for it.

medicalTreatments.name: Isotonic intravenous crystalloids
medicalTreatments.description: Balanced crystalloids (Hartmann's solution, Plasmalyte) or 0.9% saline are infused to restore intravascular volume. Balanced solutions are preferred: in the SMART trial (n=15,802), patients receiving balanced crystalloids had a 1.1% absolute reduction in major adverse kidney events compared with saline.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Balanced crystalloids reduce major adverse kidney events by approximately 1-2% compared with 0.9% saline in critically ill patients (SMART trial, 2018).

medicalTreatments.name: Blood transfusion (packed red blood cells)
medicalTreatments.description: For haemorrhagic hypovolemia with haemoglobin below 70-80 g/L (or higher thresholds in cardiovascular disease), packed red blood cells restore oxygen-carrying capacity alongside volume. In trauma, given with plasma and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio under damage-control resuscitation protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Damage-control resuscitation protocols reduce 30-day mortality by approximately 20-25% compared with crystalloid-heavy approaches in major haemorrhage (multiple military and civilian trials).

medicalTreatments.name: Tranexamic acid
medicalTreatments.description: An antifibrinolytic agent that inhibits plasmin formation, reducing ongoing haemorrhage when given within 3 hours of injury. Standard dose is 1 g IV over 10 minutes, then 1 g over 8 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the CRASH-2 trial (n=20,211), tranexamic acid given within 1 hour of injury reduced risk of death from haemorrhage by 32% (RR 0.68); benefit was attenuated when given after 3 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Vasopressors (noradrenaline)
medicalTreatments.description: In sepsis-associated or distributive hypovolemia, noradrenaline (norepinephrine) is added when volume resuscitation alone cannot achieve a mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg or higher. It restores perfusion pressure without the fluid excess that can worsen lung and gut function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Noradrenaline achieves a target MAP of ≥65 mmHg in approximately 85-90% of septic shock patients when combined with adequate fluid resuscitation per Surviving Sepsis Campaign data.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medicalTreatments.description: For mild to moderate dehydration-associated hypovolemia, WHO-standard ORS containing 75 mmol/L sodium and glucose facilitates sodium-glucose co-transport across the gut epithelium, enhancing water absorption even during active diarrhoea. It is the cornerstone of cholera and paediatric gastroenteritis management worldwide.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ORS reduces mortality from cholera-associated dehydration by approximately 70% and reduces the need for IV therapy in mild-moderate paediatric dehydration by about 50% compared with no treatment (WHO/UNICEF data).

medicalTreatments.name: Albumin solution (4% or 20%)
medicalTreatments.description: A colloid used in selected patients (e.g. spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhosis, or large-volume paracentesis) to maintain oncotic pressure and intravascular volume when crystalloids alone are insufficient or when the mechanism involves severe protein loss.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In cirrhosis-related hypovolemia, albumin infusion reduces 30-day mortality after spontaneous bacterial peritonitis by approximately 66% (Sort et al., NEJM 1999).

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical haemostasis
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency laparotomy, thoracotomy, or pelvic packing for uncontrolled internal haemorrhage; damage-control surgery prioritises achieving haemostasis and contamination control rapidly, leaving definitive repair for a second procedure after physiological stabilisation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Damage-control laparotomy in class III-IV haemorrhagic shock achieves initial haemostasis in approximately 85-92% of cases when performed promptly; overall mortality depends on injury severity and pre-operative haemodynamic status.

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular haemorrhage control
surgicalOptions.description: Interventional radiology techniques including angioembolisation for solid organ, pelvic, or gastrointestinal bleeding, and REBOA (Resuscitative Endovascular Balloon Occlusion of the Aorta) as a bridge to definitive haemostasis in haemodynamically unstable patients not yet in the operating theatre.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Angioembolisation achieves clinical success (cessation of bleeding) in 70-95% of cases depending on vessel and underlying pathology; REBOA provides temporary aortic occlusion in over 90% of attempts in experienced centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral rehydration and dietary electrolytes
alternativeTreatments.description: For mild volume depletion from heat or exercise, consuming water with electrolyte-containing foods (bananas, broth, sports drinks) restores balance without medical intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Positioning (Trendelenburg or passive leg raise)
alternativeTreatments.description: Elevating the legs 45 degrees transiently autotransfuses 150-300 mL of venous blood centrally; used as a bridge and a diagnostic test, not a definitive treatment, while IV access is established.

whySeeSpecialist: Mild dehydration can be managed in primary care or at home, but any haemodynamic instability, suspected internal haemorrhage, altered mental status, or failure to improve with basic oral hydration warrants emergency assessment. An endocrinologist should be involved when hypovolemia is recurrent and linked to adrenal insufficiency, diabetes insipidus, or renal salt-wasting. Intensivists manage resuscitation in sepsis, burns, and the post-surgical setting. Early specialist involvement reduces time-to-haemostasis in trauma and improves fluid titration accuracy, both of which are linked to survival.
doctorSelectionGuide: For emergency hypovolemia, the treating emergency physician or trauma surgeon manages acute resuscitation. Outpatient workup of recurrent volume depletion should involve an endocrinologist if adrenal or renal hormonal causes are suspected. Ask whether the clinician uses goal-directed fluid therapy protocols and point-of-care ultrasound, as these are markers of modern, evidence-based volume management. For chronic conditions such as Addison's disease or diabetes insipidus, confirm the clinician has experience with hormonal replacement titration.
preventionStrategies: Ensure adequate oral fluid intake throughout the day — adults typically need 30-35 mL/kg/day under baseline conditions, with significantly more during illness, heat, or exercise.
Replace fluids and electrolytes proactively during gastrointestinal illness, using ORS rather than plain water for significant vomiting or diarrhoea.
In patients on diuretics, monitor electrolytes and fluid status regularly and adjust doses during periods of reduced oral intake or intercurrent illness.
Manage diabetes mellitus tightly to prevent osmotic diuresis; sick-day rules should include early medical review when oral intake falls.
Use structured transfusion and fluid management protocols during major surgery and in trauma care to prevent both under- and over-resuscitation.
Optimise mineralocorticoid replacement therapy in patients with Addison's disease, and provide clear sick-day guidance for dose doubling during illness or stress.
lifestyleModifications: Drink 6-8 cups of fluids daily under normal conditions; increase intake significantly in hot weather or during physical activity.
Monitor urine colour as a simple hydration guide — pale straw is adequate; dark amber indicates the need for more fluid.
Carry an electrolyte solution when exercising intensely for more than one hour; plain water alone does not replace sodium losses from sweat.
Avoid alcohol in excess, particularly in hot weather, as it increases urine output and blunts the thirst response.
Elderly patients should set timed reminders to drink, as the sensation of thirst becomes less reliable with age.
Patients with adrenal insufficiency should wear a medical alert bracelet and keep an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit for use during crises.

recommended: Water and electrolyte-balanced fluids as the primary hydration source
Sodium-containing foods during recovery from significant dehydration — broth, soups, salted crackers
Potassium-rich foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes) to replace concomitant electrolyte losses
WHO oral rehydration solution for moderate dehydration in children and adults unable to tolerate normal diet
avoid: Large volumes of plain water without electrolytes during severe dehydration, which can dilute serum sodium
Alcohol and caffeinated drinks during recovery, as both increase renal water losses
High-sugar sports drinks in infants or during significant illness, where electrolyte composition matters more than carbohydrate content
Relying solely on thirst as a hydration guide in elderly patients, infants, or during intense exercise

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise-associated hypovolemia is preventable with adequate pre-hydration (500 mL of water in the hour before moderate exercise) and regular fluid replacement during activity (150-250 mL every 15-20 minutes during sustained exertion). After any episode of significant hypovolemia requiring medical treatment, return to strenuous exercise should be delayed until vital signs are fully normalised, electrolytes are corrected, and a clinician has cleared the patient. Patients with Addison's disease should increase their hydrocortisone dose before prolonged or strenuous exercise as per their endocrinologist's guidance.
dailyManagement: Monitor urine output and colour daily if at high risk of volume depletion.
Follow sick-day rules: during illness with reduced oral intake, contact a clinician early for guidance on IV fluid needs.
Patients with adrenal insufficiency should double their oral hydrocortisone dose during febrile illness and have injectable hydrocortisone available.
Weigh daily at the same time for patients on diuretics or with heart failure; a weight gain or loss exceeding 2 kg overnight warrants clinician review.
Carry ORS sachets when travelling to regions with high gastroenteritis risk.
prognosis: The outcome from hypovolemia depends on the severity and rapidity of volume loss, the speed of recognition and treatment, and the patient's underlying health. Mild to moderate hypovolemia from dehydration, treated with oral or intravenous fluids before organ dysfunction develops, carries an excellent prognosis with complete recovery expected. Severe haemorrhagic shock (class III-IV) carries mortality rates of 20-50% depending on injury mechanism, time-to-haemostasis, and associated injuries. Septic shock with a distributive component has an overall mortality of approximately 20-35% in high-income intensive care settings. Patients who survive significant hypovolemia without evidence of acute kidney injury, myocardial injury, or prolonged cerebral hypoperfusion typically recover full organ function. Acute kidney injury complicating hypovolemia resolves in the majority of cases with adequate resuscitation but occasionally progresses to chronic kidney disease in those with pre-existing renal impairment or delayed treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Mild dehydration resolves within 12-24 hours of adequate oral rehydration. Moderate intravenous resuscitation for isolated gastrointestinal fluid losses typically normalises vital signs and electrolytes within 24-48 hours, with discharge possible in 48-72 hours. Recovery from severe haemorrhagic shock is protracted: the acute resuscitation phase lasts 24-48 hours, an ICU stay of days to weeks may follow for monitoring and management of complications, and full recovery of function can take weeks to months, particularly after major surgery or multisystem trauma. Burn-associated hypovolemia requires structured resuscitation over the first 24-48 hours; the overall recovery from major burns extends over months.
complications: Acute kidney injury — prerenal azotaemia from sustained hypovolemia can progress to acute tubular necrosis; oliguria beyond 6-12 hours without response to fluids warrants nephrology review.

Cardiac ischaemia and arrhythmia — reduced coronary perfusion in the setting of tachycardia increases myocardial oxygen demand while reducing supply; patients with coronary artery disease are particularly vulnerable.
Multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) — prolonged hypoperfusion initiates a systemic inflammatory cascade that can impair liver, lung, gut, and brain function even after volume restoration.
Ischaemic stroke or watershed brain injury — cerebral hypoperfusion, particularly in elderly patients or those with carotid stenosis, can produce focal or diffuse neurological deficits.

Abdominal compartment syndrome — aggressive fluid resuscitation, especially in burns or major abdominal surgery, causes bowel oedema and raised intra-abdominal pressure that worsens organ perfusion in a vicious cycle.

Electrolyte disturbances — rapid fluid shifts cause hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, hypocalcaemia, or hyperchloraemic acidosis depending on the fluid type used and the underlying mechanism of volume loss.

supportResources.name: NHS — Dehydration
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of dehydration symptoms, prevention, and when to seek medical care.

supportResources.name: CDC — Heat Stress and Hydration
supportResources.description: Occupational health guidance on fluid needs in hot environments to prevent heat-related hypovolemia.

supportResources.name: WHO — Oral Rehydration Salts
supportResources.description: WHO guidance on oral rehydration therapy for dehydration management in community settings.

sources.title: Cannon JW. Hemorrhagic Shock. New England Journal of Medicine, 2018
CRASH-2 trial collaborators. Effects of tranexamic acid on death, vascular occlusive events, and blood transfusion. Lancet, 2010
Semler MW et al. Balanced Crystalloids versus Saline in Critically Ill Adults (SMART). NEJM, 2018
WHO and UNICEF. Oral Rehydration Salts: Production of the New ORS. WHO, 2006
American College of Surgeons. ATLS Advanced Trauma Life Support Student Course Manual, 10th ed. ACS, 2018

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure is a pump failure causing fluid overload and oedema; hypovolemia is a volume deficit. Both can cause reduced urine output but heart failure shows raised JVP and pulmonary congestion rather than flat veins and dry mucous membranes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity without necessarily reducing total blood volume; tachycardia and pallor overlap with hypovolemia, but anaemia alone does not cause orthostatic hypotension or raised urea-to-creatinine ratios unless haemorrhagic.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute kidney injury from intrinsic renal disease also causes oliguria and raised creatinine, but urine sodium is high (above 40 mmol/L) and urine is poorly concentrated, the opposite of the picture in volume-depleted prerenal states.

relatedConditions.name: Acute kidney injury
relatedConditions.relevance: Sustained hypovolemia is the commonest cause of acute kidney injury; prompt volume restoration prevents progression from prerenal azotaemia to tubular necrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Diuretic over-treatment of heart failure is a frequent cause of hypovolemia; conversely, the haemodynamic picture of acute cardiac failure can mimic hypovolemia, making echocardiography essential.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Poorly controlled type 2 diabetes causes osmotic diuresis that produces progressive volume depletion, which can precipitate acute kidney injury and hyerosmolar hyperglycaemic state.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Massive urinary protein loss lowers oncotic pressure, promoting intravascular hypovolemia despite total body sodium overload — a challenging management scenario requiring albumin and carefully titrated diuresis.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe haemorrhagic hypovolemia produces acute anaemia; conversely, chronic anaemia reduces the buffer capacity when any additional volume loss occurs.

faqs.question: What is hypovolemia and how is it different from dehydration?
faqs.answer: Hypovolemia means the volume of circulating blood is too low to maintain normal perfusion. Dehydration is one cause, occurring when total body water falls. Hypovolemia can also result from haemorrhage or fluid shifts into tissues without any water deficit. All dehydration causes hypovolemia but not all hypovolemia is dehydration.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of low blood volume?
faqs.answer: The earliest signs are a faster-than-usual resting heart rate, darker and less frequent urination, dry mouth, and mild lightheadedness when standing quickly. These appear before blood pressure falls and indicate the body is compensating for reduced circulating volume.

faqs.question: How much fluid loss causes hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: A loss of approximately 10% of circulating blood volume — about 500 mL in an average adult — begins to trigger compensatory responses. A 15-20% loss produces mild to moderate symptoms. A loss exceeding 30-40% (1.5-2 litres) causes shock with falling blood pressure and requires emergency resuscitation.

faqs.question: When is hypovolemia a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if systolic blood pressure drops below 90 mmHg, heart rate exceeds 120 beats per minute at rest, there is visible major bleeding, consciousness is altered, or an infant or child has no urine output for more than 6 hours. These indicate class III-IV shock requiring immediate IV fluid and medical intervention.

faqs.question: What IV fluid is used to treat hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: Balanced crystalloid solutions such as Hartmann's (lactated Ringer's) or Plasmalyte are first-line for most causes of hypovolemia. Normal saline (0.9% NaCl) is an alternative but can cause hyperchloraemic acidosis in large volumes. Haemorrhagic shock also requires red blood cell transfusion; colloids such as albumin are reserved for specific situations.

faqs.question: Can hypovolemia cause kidney damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sustained hypovolemia reduces blood flow to the kidneys, causing prerenal acute kidney injury. If untreated for hours, it progresses to acute tubular necrosis. Most cases recover with prompt fluid resuscitation, but patients with pre-existing kidney disease or prolonged hypoperfusion are at risk of permanent damage.

faqs.question: How do doctors tell how severe hypovolemia is?
faqs.answer: Severity is assessed using vital signs, mental status, urine output, and blood lactate. The ATLS classification grades haemorrhagic shock from class I (up to 15% blood loss, mild tachycardia) to class IV (over 40% blood loss, collapse). A lactate above 4 mmol/L with haemodynamic instability indicates critical perfusion failure.

faqs.question: What causes hypovolemia other than bleeding?
faqs.answer: Non-haemorrhagic causes include severe vomiting or diarrhoea, burns, sepsis with capillary leak, excessive diuretic use, uncontrolled diabetes with osmotic diuresis, adrenal insufficiency, and third-space fluid sequestration in pancreatitis or bowel obstruction.

faqs.question: Does drinking water fix hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: Plain water treats mild dehydration-associated hypovolemia effectively. For more than mild loss, electrolytes must be replaced alongside water — WHO oral rehydration solution is ideal. Severe hypovolemia from haemorrhage or sepsis requires intravenous fluids and treatment of the underlying cause; drinking water alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: Can hypovolemia be prevented?
faqs.answer: Many cases are preventable. Adequate daily fluid intake, proactive replacement during illness or heat, careful monitoring of patients on diuretics, and structured surgical fluid protocols reduce risk. In Addison's disease, consistent mineralocorticoid replacement and sick-day dose adjustments are critical prevention measures.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypovolemia and hyponatraemia?
faqs.answer: Hypovolemia is a volume deficit; hyponatraemia is a low sodium concentration. Both can occur together or separately. A patient who loses sodium-rich fluids and replaces them with pure water develops hyponatraemia without correcting hypovolemia. Treatment requires both volume and the appropriate sodium concentration.

faqs.question: How quickly does the body recover from hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: Mild dehydration resolves within 12-24 hours of oral rehydration. Moderate hypovolemia requiring intravenous fluids typically normalises within 24-48 hours. Severe haemorrhagic shock or septic shock requires days in intensive care, and full recovery of organ function can take weeks. Residual kidney injury may persist if there was prolonged hypoperfusion.

faqs.question: Is low blood pressure always present with hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: No. Blood pressure can remain within the normal range until 20-30% of circulating volume is lost, because the body compensates with tachycardia and vasoconstriction. A normal blood pressure does not exclude significant volume depletion. Orthostatic vital signs and urine output are more sensitive early indicators than lying blood pressure alone.

faqs.question: What does hypovolemia do to the heart?
faqs.answer: Reduced preload decreases cardiac output per stroke. The heart compensates by beating faster (tachycardia). In sustained severe hypovolemia, the combination of tachycardia and reduced coronary perfusion increases the risk of myocardial ischaemia, particularly in patients with existing coronary artery disease.

faqs.question: Why is tranexamic acid given in haemorrhagic shock?
faqs.answer: Major bleeding triggers the body's own clot-dissolving system (fibrinolysis) in an uncontrolled way, which worsens bleeding. Tranexamic acid blocks this process. The CRASH-2 trial showed it reduces haemorrhage-related death by 32% when given within 1 hour of injury, making it a standard part of major trauma resuscitation.

faqs.question: What urine output level indicates hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: Output below 0.5 mL per kilogram per hour in adults suggests inadequate renal perfusion from volume depletion. Below 0.3 mL/kg/hour indicates severe hypoperfusion. A urinary catheter is placed in all critically ill patients to monitor output continuously as a real-time indicator of resuscitation adequacy.

faqs.question: Can someone with hypovolemia have normal kidney tests?
faqs.answer: Early in mild hypovolemia, serum creatinine may be normal because kidneys have large reserve. The urea-to-creatinine ratio rises first, followed by creatinine if hypoperfusion continues. Absence of creatinine elevation should not reassure if other markers suggest volume depletion.

faqs.question: What is permissive hypotension in trauma?
faqs.answer: In active uncontrolled haemorrhage, targeting a systolic blood pressure of 80-90 mmHg rather than normal is deliberate. Aggressive fluid resuscitation to normal BP before bleeding is controlled dilutes clotting factors and worsens coagulopathy. Once haemostasis is secured, normal blood pressure targets are restored.

faqs.question: How does adrenal insufficiency cause hypovolemia?
faqs.answer: The adrenal glands produce aldosterone, which signals the kidneys to retain sodium and water. In Addison's disease, aldosterone is deficient. Without it, excessive sodium and water are lost in the urine. This combined renal salt-wasting causes progressive volume depletion that can precipitate adrenal crisis with severe hypotension.

faqs.question: What is the Parkland formula for burn resuscitation?
faqs.answer: The Parkland formula estimates fluid replacement for burn injuries: 4 mL per kilogram of body weight per percentage of total body surface area burned over 24 hours, with half given in the first 8 hours and half over the next 16. It is a starting estimate; actual volumes are adjusted based on urine output and clinical response.

metaDescription: Hypovolemia means low blood volume from bleeding, dehydration or burns. Learn symptoms, emergency signs and IV fluid treatments. Doctor-reviewed guide.
